specification.topic-statement: The REPL event handler system drives the select-based event loop by processing terminal input, HTTP completion events, and timer events to advance agent state and update the display.

scope.in-scope: Select timeout calculation, file descriptor set population, terminal byte reading and action dispatch, HTTP completion event processing per agent (including success, error, and interrupt paths), assistant message persistence, tool loop continuation, select timeout handling for scroll detector flushing, and minimum curl timeout calculation.
scope.boundary: Terminal bytes are parsed and dispatched by calling the action dispatcher. Action handling behavior is external to this topic.

HTTP I/O is driven by calling the provider's perform, info_read, fdset, and timeout operations. Stream and completion callbacks registered earlier fire during info_read. The internal mechanics are external to this topic.
Agent idle flag, message persistence, and LISTEN/NOTIFY broadcasts are performed via database helpers. Errors are silently ignored.
Rendering individual scrollback events, storing response metadata, rendering usage events, and extracting tool calls are handled by external helpers called during request success processing.

behavior.select-timeout-calculation:
The minimum positive timeout in milliseconds is chosen from: - 80 ms if the current agent's spinner is visible - 50 ms if any agent is in EXECUTING_TOOL state (agent states read atomically) - The scroll detector's pending-flush window (if a scroll detector exists) - The caller-supplied curl recommended timeout
If all candidate values are negative (none applicable), 1000 ms is used. Agent states are read atomically to avoid data races.
behavior.fd-set-population: All three fd_sets (read, write, except) are zeroed. The terminal file descriptor is added to the read set if it is non-negative. The control socket adds its own descriptors via its own helper. For each agent with an active provider, that provider's fdset operation populates the sets. The highest file descriptor number seen is reported. If any provider's fdset operation returns an error, the function returns that error without processing remaining agents.
behavior.terminal-input-handling:
One byte is read from the terminal file descriptor. If the read is interrupted by a signal (EINTR), the function returns success. If the read returns zero or a negative value not due to a signal, the exit flag is set to true and the function returns success (signaling normal shutdown).
On a successful byte read, the current CLOCK_MONOTONIC time is recorded as the render start timestamp. The byte is fed to the input parser to produce an action. The action is dispatched. If the action type is not unknown, a full frame render is triggered.
behavior.curl-event-processing:
For each agent in the agents array, HTTP completion events are processed. After processing the array, the current agent is processed again (to handle cases where it may not be in the array).
If the agent has no active HTTP request or no provider, processing is skipped.
The previous in-flight count is saved, then the provider's perform operation drives HTTP I/O and updates the in-flight count. The provider's info_read operation processes any completed transfers (invoking the registered completion callback for each).
If the in-flight count is still positive or it was already zero before the call, no request has just completed — the function returns.
If the agent's state is not WAITING_FOR_LLM (read atomically), the function returns without handling the completion.
If the agent's interrupt-requested flag is true, interrupted LLM completion handling runs: - The interrupt flag is cleared. - Any partial assistant response and error message are freed. - The message array is scanned backward to find the most recent user message. All messages from that point forward are marked as interrupted. - The scrollback is cleared and rebuilt by re-rendering every message in order. - An "interrupted" record is persisted to the database. - The agent transitions to IDLE and the idle notification is broadcast. - If the agent is the current agent, a frame render is triggered.
If an HTTP error message is present on the agent, it is prepended with "Error: " and appended to the scrollback as a single line. Both the error message and any partial assistant response are freed and set to null. Error messages are not persisted to the database.
If no error message is present:
If the agent has a non-empty assistant response string, a text message with assistant role is created and added to the in-memory conversation. The response is then persisted to the database as an "assistant" message with metadata (provider, model, thinking level, finish reason). If token counts are positive, a "usage" record is also persisted. Database errors are silently ignored.
The assistant response string is freed and set to null.
If a pending tool call exists on the agent, tool execution is started and processing returns without continuing to the tool-loop check.
If the tool loop should continue (checked against max iterations and finish reason), the tool iteration counter is incremented and a continuation request is submitted to the provider.
If the agent's state is still WAITING_FOR_LLM after success or error handling: - Token counts are recorded and the cache is pruned (on success only). - The agent transitions to IDLE. - The idle flag is updated in the database and the idle notification is broadcast. If the agent is the current agent, a frame render is triggered.
behavior.select-timeout-event-handling: If a scroll detector exists, the current CLOCK_MONOTONIC time is read. The scroll detector's timeout check is called. If the result is a pending arrow-up, the arrow-up viewport action is triggered and a frame render follows. If the result is a pending arrow-down, the same for arrow-down.
behavior.curl-timeout-calculation: All agents with active providers are queried for their recommended HTTP timeout. The minimum non-negative value across all agents is returned. If no agents have active providers, -1 is returned (no timeout needed).
behavior.idle-notification: After any completion (success, error, or interrupt), if a database context is present, the agent's idle flag is set to true in the database and a notification is broadcast on the channel "agent_event_{uuid}" with the payload "idle". Errors are silently ignored.
